meta.title: [IRS] New Mix Questions
meta.description: A collection of Information Retrieval Systems exam questions.

questions.questionMd: A corpus contains 1000 documents, and the term "quasar" appears in exactly 50 of them. Applying the formula `IDF(t) = log(N / df(t))`, what is the IDF of "quasar"?
questions.explanationMd: Substitute into the formula: `N / df = 1000 / 50 = 20`. Therefore `IDF = log(20)`, which is approximately **3.0** when using the natural logarithm. The key step is computing the ratio `N / df`, not `df / N`.

questions.questionMd: How does BM25's approach to document length normalization differ from the cosine-based L2 normalization used in the Vector Space Model?

options.label: A
options.text: BM25 normalizes by the document's maximum term frequency, while L2 normalization normalizes by the sum of all term frequencies

options.label: B
options.text: BM25 applies per-term length normalization controlled by the parameter b

options.label: C
options.text: BM25 applies length normalization only to query terms, whereas L2 normalization is applied only to document vectors

options.label: D
options.text: BM25 removes length normalization entirely, relying solely on TF saturation to control the influence of document length

questions.explanationMd: BM25 uses a **document-length normalization factor** inside its scoring formula, controlled by the parameter `b` and relative to `avgdl`. L2 normalization in the Vector Space Model instead rescales the **entire vector** to unit length. So BM25 and cosine normalization both address length effects, but they do so in **different mathematical ways**.

questions.questionMd: What simplifying assumption does the Binary Independence Model (BIM) make about query terms when estimating the probability that a document is relevant?

options.label: A
options.text: Terms are assumed to appear the same number of times in every relevant document in the collection

options.label: B
options.text: Terms are assumed to be statistically independent of one another given the relevance status of the document

options.label: C
options.text: Terms are assumed to have equal document frequency across the entire collection

options.label: D
options.text: Terms are assumed to be ranked by their IDF value before any probability estimation is performed

questions.explanationMd: The **Binary Independence Model** assumes that query terms are conditionally independent given relevance or non-relevance. This assumption makes the probability model tractable and is one of the foundations behind probabilistic IR and BM25-style reasoning.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture notes, in which of the following systems has BM25 been the default scorer?

options.label: A
options.text: The TREC evaluation framework as the only permitted baseline for ad-hoc retrieval tracks

options.label: B
options.text: Elasticsearch and OpenSearch since version 5.0, and Lucene since version 6.0

options.label: C
options.text: The Google PageRank algorithm as its primary term-matching component since 2010

options.label: D
options.text: Apache Spark MLlib and scikit-learn since their initial releases

questions.explanationMd: BM25 became the default ranking function in major Lucene-based search systems. The key point is that **Lucene and systems built on it adopted BM25 as the standard lexical scorer**, replacing older defaults such as classic TF-IDF similarity.

questions.questionMd: Which statement correctly describes a key theoretical difference between BM25 and TF-IDF with cosine similarity?

options.label: A
options.text: Both BM25 and TF-IDF are derived from the Binary Independence Model but differ only in their length normalization

options.label: B
options.text: TF-IDF is derived from the Probability Ranking Principle, while BM25 is an empirical approximation with no theoretical basis

options.label: C
options.text: BM25 is derived from the Binary Independence Model and the Probability Ranking Principle, while TF-IDF is a heuristic weighting scheme

options.label: D
options.text: BM25 is a purely heuristic method, while TF-IDF has a formal probabilistic derivation from the Robertson-Sparck Jones weight

questions.explanationMd: BM25 has a **probabilistic motivation** connected to the Binary Independence Model and Probability Ranking Principle. By contrast, TF-IDF is mainly a **heuristic weighting family** rather than a single formally derived probabilistic model.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following is a known limitation of the probabilistic IDF formula `log((N - df) / df)` as noted in the lecture?

options.label: A
options.text: The formula cannot be computed when the document frequency is greater than 1

options.label: B
options.text: The formula doubles the IDF value of any term whose document frequency exceeds N/2

options.label: C
options.text: The formula always returns the same value regardless of the collection size N

options.label: D
options.text: The formula can produce negative values, requiring careful handling in implementations

questions.explanationMd: If `df > N/2`, then `(N - df) / df < 1`, and the logarithm becomes **negative**. That is a known practical issue with this probabilistic form of IDF and motivates smoothing or clipping strategies in implementations.

questions.questionMd: After L2 normalization is applied to all document vectors in the Vector Space Model, what geometric property do all normalized document vectors share?

options.label: A
options.text: All normalized document vectors have the same direction as the query vector for the collection's most common query

options.label: B
options.text: All normalized document vectors become orthogonal to one another, eliminating redundant documents

options.label: C
options.text: All normalized document vectors are projected onto a two-dimensional plane to enable fast ranking

options.label: D
options.text: All normalized document vectors lie on the unit sphere, meaning their Euclidean norm equals 1

questions.explanationMd: L2 normalization divides each vector by its Euclidean norm. As a result, every normalized vector has **length 1**, so all such vectors lie on the **unit sphere** in vector space.

questions.questionMd: In the SMART notation table, the augmented TF variant (symbol `a`) is defined as which of the following?

options.label: A
options.text: tf / (tf + max_tf(d))

options.label: B
options.text: 0.5 + 0.5 * tf / max_tf(d)

options.label: C
options.text: 1 + log(tf / max_tf(d))

options.label: D
options.text: 0.5 * log(tf) + 0.5 * max_tf(d)

questions.explanationMd: The SMART **augmented TF** rescales term frequency relative to the document's maximum term frequency: `0.5 + 0.5 * tf / max_tf(d)`. This keeps weights in a moderate range and reduces the dominance of raw counts.

questions.questionMd: Which statement best captures the concept of TF saturation as modeled by BM25 and log-TF?

options.label: A
options.text: TF saturation increases the weight of the first occurrence of a term and sets all subsequent occurrences to zero

options.label: B
options.text: TF saturation means that once a term appears more than `k1` times, it is completely ignored in scoring

options.label: C
options.text: Each additional occurrence of a term contributes equally to the relevance score regardless of how many times it has already appeared

options.label: D
options.text: Each additional occurrence of a term contributes progressively less to the relevance score

questions.explanationMd: TF saturation means there are **diminishing returns**. The first few occurrences of a term usually matter more than later repetitions, so BM25 and log-TF both dampen the gain from very large raw term counts.

questions.questionMd: As the raw term frequency `tf` in a document increases toward infinity, what value does the BM25 TF saturation function `tf * (k1 + 1) / (tf + k1)` approach?

options.label: A
options.text: The value `k1`, which acts as a hard ceiling on TF contributions

options.label: B
options.text: The value `tf`, meaning the saturation function converges back to raw TF at very high counts

options.label: C
options.text: The value `1`, because the saturation function is normalized to the range [0, 1]

options.label: D
options.text: The value `k1 + 1`, which is the asymptotic maximum that the saturation function never exceeds

questions.explanationMd: For very large `tf`, the highest-degree terms dominate and the fraction approaches `k1 + 1`. So the BM25 TF component has a **finite asymptote**, which is how it enforces saturation.

questions.questionMd: Why is raw term frequency (TF) considered a biased signal when comparing documents of different lengths?

options.label: A
options.text: Raw TF values become negative for very high-frequency terms

options.label: B
options.text: Longer documents tend to accumulate higher raw TF values

options.label: C
options.text: Raw TF counts cannot be stored in an inverted index

options.label: D
options.text: Raw TF ignores whether a term appears in the query at all

questions.explanationMd: Longer documents naturally have more opportunities to repeat a term, so raw TF can unfairly favor them. This is why many weighting schemes normalize or damp TF before using it in ranking.

questions.questionMd: Using the standard IDF formula taught in lecture, how is `IDF(t)` computed for a term `t` given a collection of `N` documents where it appears in `df(t)` of them?

options.label: A
options.text: log(df(t) / N)

options.label: B
options.text: N / df(t)

options.label: C
options.text: log(N / df(t))

options.label: D
options.text: log(N + df(t))

questions.explanationMd: The standard IDF idea is to compare the total number of documents `N` with the number containing the term `df(t)`. That gives `IDF(t) = log(N / df(t))`, which increases as the term becomes rarer.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following is a fundamental limitation of Boolean retrieval that motivates the move to ranked retrieval?

options.label: A
options.text: Boolean retrieval cannot use an inverted index to look up postings lists

options.label: B
options.text: Boolean retrieval requires all query terms to be stemmed before matching

options.label: C
options.text: Boolean retrieval is unable to handle OR operators between query terms

options.label: D
options.text: A document matching 3 out of 4 query terms is assumed no better than a document matching 0 terms

questions.explanationMd: Boolean retrieval makes **hard yes/no decisions**. Without ranking, it cannot express that one document is *almost* a match while another is completely irrelevant. That inability to capture **partial matching quality** is a major reason ranked retrieval became standard.

questions.questionMd: Which expression correctly gives the cosine similarity between document vector `d` and query vector `q`?
questions.explanationMd: Cosine similarity is the **dot product divided by the product of vector norms**. This makes the score depend on the angle between the vectors rather than their raw magnitudes.

questions.questionMd: According to the length-normalization worked example in the lecture notes, when two documents contain a query term the same number of times but differ in length, how does BM25 treat the shorter document?

options.label: A
options.text: The shorter document is excluded from ranking because BM25 requires a minimum document length relative to `avgdl`

options.label: B
options.text: The shorter document receives a lower TF saturation score because BM25 penalizes documents with few total tokens

options.label: C
options.text: Both documents receive identical TF saturation scores because BM25 only compares document length to `k1`, not to `avgdl`

options.label: D
options.text: The shorter document receives a higher TF saturation score because its length is below average, signaling greater topical focus

questions.explanationMd: BM25 rewards term occurrences **more strongly in shorter documents** when all else is equal, because the same number of matches in fewer tokens suggests greater topical concentration. This is one of the main effects of BM25's document-length normalization.

questions.questionMd: A developer wants to build a faceted navigation sidebar that counts how many products belong to each category (e.g., "Electronics: 120", "Clothing: 85"). Which field type should the `category` field use in the OpenSearch mapping?

options.label: A
options.text: integer, because aggregation requires numeric fields to produce accurate bucket counts

options.label: B
options.text: text, because it supports full-text analysis which enables partial matching of category names

options.label: C
options.text: text with the english analyzer, so that category names are stemmed before aggregation

options.label: D
options.text: keyword, because it stores exact unanalyzed values that can be used in terms aggregations

questions.explanationMd: Faceted navigation usually needs **exact bucket labels**, not analyzed text. A `keyword` field preserves the original category value such as `Electronics` or `Clothing`, so OpenSearch can count exact matches in a **terms aggregation**. A `text` field would be analyzed into tokens, which is useful for full-text search but not for exact category buckets.

questions.questionMd: Why do high-frequency terms with large document frequency achieve better compression ratios than rare terms when gap encoding is combined with variable-byte encoding?

options.label: A
options.text: High-frequency terms are processed first by SPIMI, so their postings lists are already sorted

options.label: B
options.text: High-frequency terms appear in many documents, producing smaller average gaps that fit in fewer VB bytes

options.label: C
options.text: High-frequency terms have shorter string representations in the dictionary, reducing total index size

options.label: D
options.text: High-frequency terms benefit from front-coding in the dictionary, independently of the postings lists

questions.explanationMd: Gap encoding stores differences between adjacent docIDs. When a term appears in many documents, those gaps tend to be smaller on average, and **small integers need fewer bytes** under variable-byte encoding. That is why frequent terms often compress better in postings lists.

questions.questionMd: The sentence "The cat sat on the mat" contains how many tokens and how many types respectively?

options.label: A
options.text: 6 tokens and 5 types

options.label: B
options.text: 6 tokens and 6 types

options.label: C
options.text: 5 tokens and 5 types

options.label: D
options.text: 5 tokens and 6 types

questions.explanationMd: There are **6 tokens** in the sentence: `The`, `cat`, `sat`, `on`, `the`, `mat`. The unique word forms are `{the, cat, sat, on, mat}`, which gives **5 types** if case is normalized in the usual way.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between a token, a type, and a term?

options.label: A
options.text: A term is one occurrence in text; a token is a unique normalized form; a type is what enters the index

options.label: B
options.text: A token is one occurrence in text; a type is a unique word form; a term is a normalized type entering the index

options.label: C
options.text: A type is one occurrence in text; a token is a unique word form; a term is produced by tokenization

options.label: D
options.text: A token and a type are interchangeable; a term is produced only after stopword removal

questions.explanationMd: A **token** is a specific occurrence in text. A **type** is the distinct word form represented by many token occurrences. A **term** is typically the normalized form that survives preprocessing and is actually stored in the index.

questions.questionMd: On a log-log plot of term rank versus term frequency, what shape does Zipf's law predict?

options.label: A
options.text: A concave curve that levels off as rank increases

options.label: B
options.text: A straight line with a slope of approximately -1

options.label: C
options.text: An S-shaped logistic curve

options.label: D
options.text: A straight line with a positive slope

questions.explanationMd: Zipf's law predicts an inverse relationship between rank and frequency. On a **log-log plot**, that power-law behavior appears approximately as a **straight line with slope -1**.

questions.questionMd: A corpus has `N = 100,000` tokens. Using Heaps' law with `k = 44` and `b = 0.49`, what is the approximate predicted vocabulary size?

options.label: A
options.text: Approximately 100,000 unique terms

options.label: B
options.text: Approximately 13,600 unique terms

options.label: C
options.text: Approximately 44,000 unique terms

options.label: D
options.text: Approximately 4,400 unique terms

questions.explanationMd: Heaps' law is `V = kN^b`. Plugging in the values gives approximately `44 * 100000^0.49`, which is about **13,600**. The key takeaway is that vocabulary growth is **sub-linear**, not proportional to corpus size.

questions.questionMd: What is the time complexity of BSBI expressed in terms of `T`, the total number of token occurrences in the corpus?

options.label: A
options.text: O(T log T) due to the dominant sorting step

options.label: B
options.text: O(T) because each token is processed exactly once in a single pass

options.label: C
options.text: O(T^2) because every token pair must be compared during the merge phase

options.label: D
options.text: O(T log N) where `N` is the number of documents

questions.explanationMd: BSBI spends most of its cost sorting term-document pairs, so the dominant complexity is **O(T log T)**. The indexing process is not purely linear because sorting is central to the method.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture's compression data, combining gap encoding with variable-byte encoding on a typical postings list achieves what overall compression factor compared to storing raw `int32` docIDs?

options.label: A
options.text: Exactly 8× compression because VB uses 7 data bits per byte instead of 8

options.label: B
options.text: Less than 1.5× compression

options.label: C
options.text: Approximately 10–20× compression

options.label: D
options.text: Approximately 3–5× compression

questions.explanationMd: In practice, gap encoding plus variable-byte encoding often gives a **moderate but significant** compression gain, commonly around **3–5×** compared with raw 32-bit docIDs. The exact value depends on the distribution of gaps.

questions.questionMd: What practical implication does Zipf's law have for the design of a stopword list?

options.label: A
options.text: Zipf's law predicts that stopword removal will have no measurable effect on vocabulary size

options.label: B
options.text: The most frequent terms visible at the left of the Zipf curve are low-information words that can often be safely removed

options.label: C
options.text: Zipf's law implies that all terms above a fixed frequency threshold carry equal information

options.label: D
options.text: Stopwords should be selected from the rarest terms at the right tail of the Zipf distribution

questions.explanationMd: Zipf's law highlights that a small set of very frequent words account for a large fraction of token occurrences. Many of these are function words with low discriminative power, so they are good candidates for a **stopword list**.

questions.questionMd: In a standard TREC qrels file, each line maps a `(query_id, doc_id)` pair to a relevance label. What does a grade of `0` in a graded qrels file signify?

options.label: A
options.text: The document was not in the judgment pool and was therefore never assessed

options.label: B
options.text: The document was judged by a human assessor and found to be irrelevant to the query

options.label: C
options.text: The document is partially relevant — useful but not a complete answer

options.label: D
options.text: The query was retired and no longer active in the evaluation campaign

questions.explanationMd: In graded qrels, a label of **0** normally means the document was **judged irrelevant**. It is different from an unjudged document, which is a separate issue in pooled evaluation.

questions.questionMd: A system achieves `NDCG@k = 1.0` for a given query. What must be true?

options.label: A
options.text: The system's DCG@k equals zero, meaning no relevant documents were returned

options.label: B
options.text: `NDCG@k = 1.0` is impossible; values are strictly bounded below 1.0 in practice

options.label: C
options.text: The system retrieved exactly `k` documents, all of which are relevant

options.label: D
options.text: The system's ranking is identical to the ideal ranking

questions.explanationMd: NDCG is computed by dividing DCG by the **ideal DCG**. A value of **1.0** means the system matched the ideal ordering exactly for the cutoff being considered.

questions.questionMd: In Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG), relevance scores are divided by the log base-2 of `(rank + 1)`. What user behavior does this logarithmic discount model?

options.label: A
options.text: The discount penalizes systems that retrieve more than `k` documents, keeping result sets short

options.label: B
options.text: Users ignore results in random order, so position should have no effect on contribution

options.label: C
options.text: Users prefer documents that are only marginally relevant at high ranks over highly relevant documents at low ranks

options.label: D
options.text: User attention diminishes at lower ranks, so a relevant document at rank 10 is worth less than one at rank 1

questions.explanationMd: DCG discounts gain as rank increases because users usually pay more attention to **higher-ranked** results. A relevant result found lower in the list is still useful, but it contributes less than one found near the top.

questions.questionMd: Which formula correctly defines the F1 score in terms of Precision (`P`) and Recall (`R`)?

options.label: A
options.text: 2PR / (P + R)

options.label: C
options.text: R / (P + R)

questions.explanationMd: The F1 score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall: `2PR / (P + R)`. It rewards systems that balance the two measures rather than maximizing only one.

questions.questionMd: Why is Normalized DCG (NDCG) preferred over raw DCG when comparing results across multiple queries?

options.label: A
options.text: NDCG applies a steeper position discount than DCG, making it more sensitive to early retrieval errors

options.label: B
options.text: NDCG normalizes by the ideal DCG so all queries map to [0, 1]

options.label: C
options.text: Raw DCG rewards retrieving more documents, while NDCG penalizes systems that return more than `k` results

options.label: D
options.text: NDCG uses binary relevance labels, which are easier to collect than the graded labels required by raw DCG

questions.explanationMd: Raw DCG values are not directly comparable across queries because queries can have very different gain distributions. NDCG divides by the **ideal DCG**, which puts scores on a shared **0 to 1 scale**.

questions.questionMd: The Ideal DCG (`IDCG@k`) is defined as the `DCG@k` of which ranking?

options.label: A
options.text: The ranking produced by the best-performing system in the evaluation pool

options.label: B
options.text: The ranking that maximizes Precision@k regardless of graded relevance

options.label: C
options.text: The ranking that places all irrelevant documents first to establish a worst-case baseline

options.label: D
options.text: The theoretically perfect ranking, where all documents are sorted by descending relevance grade

questions.explanationMd: IDCG is the DCG of the **best possible ordering** for that query under the available relevance labels. It is obtained by sorting documents by **descending relevance grade**.

questions.questionMd: Given the ranked list `R, I, I, R, R` (`R = relevant`, `I = irrelevant`), what is the Average Precision (AP)?
questions.explanationMd: Relevant documents appear at ranks 1, 4, and 5. The precision values there are `1/1 = 1.0`, `2/4 = 0.5`, and `3/5 = 0.6`. Averaging them gives `(1.0 + 0.5 + 0.6)/3 = 0.7`, so **AP = 0.700**.

questions.questionMd: Three queries yield Average Precision scores of `0.72`, `0.45`, and `0.38` for a system. What is the Mean Average Precision (MAP)?

options.label: A
options.text: 0.720, because MAP reports the highest AP score across all queries

options.label: C
options.text: 0.550, computed as the median of the three AP values

options.label: D
options.text: 1.550, because MAP sums the AP values without dividing by the number of queries

questions.explanationMd: MAP is simply the **mean of AP over queries**. Here that is `(0.72 + 0.45 + 0.38) / 3 = 0.5166...`, which rounds to **0.517**.

questions.questionMd: In SMART notation, what does the scheme `lnc` specify for document weighting?

options.label: A
options.text: Natural TF, no IDF, cosine normalization

options.label: B
options.text: Log-average TF, natural IDF, no normalization

options.label: C
options.text: Logarithmic TF, natural IDF, cosine normalization

options.label: D
options.text: Logarithmic TF, no IDF, cosine normalization

questions.explanationMd: In SMART notation, `l` means **logarithmic TF**, `n` means **no IDF**, and `c` means **cosine normalization**. So `lnc` corresponds to **log TF + no IDF + cosine normalization**.
